1: A theme: "Ascribe to the LORD, O heavenly beings (bene elim), ascribe to the LORD glory and strength." The heavenly beings summoned to worship — the divine council, the angelic host, the "sons of God" of Genesis 6:2 and Job 1:6 — are called to direct their worship to YHWH. The NT extends this command to its Christological fulfillment: Hebrews 1:6 (citing Psalms 97:7 LXX) says that at the introduction of the firstborn into the world, God commands: "Let all God's angels worship him." The worship that Psalm 29 commands the heavenly beings to direct to YHWH is commanded in Hebrews to be directed toward the Son — the strongest NT statement of Christ's divine identity.
2: A theme: "Give the LORD the glory due his name; worship the LORD in the splendor of his holiness." The "glory due his name" — the kavod that properly belongs to YHWH — is what the NT presents as belonging to Christ. John 17:5 (Jesus: "glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began"), John 12:41 (Isaiah saw Christ's glory in the Isa 6 throne-vision), and the heavenly doxologies of Revelation (Psalms 5:12-13; Psalms 7:12) all extend the "glory due his name" to include Christ within the identity of the divine name. Psalm 29's call to worship in the "splendor of holiness" is ultimately a call to recognize and worship the God who is fully revealed in Christ.
3: A theme: "The voice of the LORD is over the waters; the God of glory thunders — the LORD, over mighty waters." The "voice of the LORD" (qol YHWH) repeated seven times in this psalm is the thunderous divine speech that governs creation and history. The NT's deepest engagement with this image is the identification of Christ as the divine Word/Logos (John 1:1-3): the voice of the LORD through whom creation came into being is the same Word who became flesh. At Jesus's baptism, the Father's voice speaks from heaven over the waters of the Jordan (Matthew 3:17; Mark 1:11; Luke 3:22) — the "voice of the LORD over the waters" given its ultimate specification: "This is my Son, whom I love."
4: A theme: "The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is majestic." The paired attributes — power (koah) and majesty (hadar) — of the divine voice are the attributes the NT assigns to Christ's own voice and word. Matthew 7:28-29: "the crowds were amazed at his teaching, because he taught as one who had authority (exousia), and not as their teachers of the law." Revelation 1:15 describes the risen Christ's voice as "like the sound of rushing waters" — the same awesome magnitude as the voice of the LORD over the waters in Psalm 29. The majestic, powerful divine voice becomes incarnate and audible in the teaching and commands of Jesus.
5: A theme: "The voice of the LORD shatters the cedars; the LORD shatters the cedars of Lebanon." The cedars of Lebanon were the symbol of human pride and greatness in the ancient Near East — the most magnificent trees, used for royal palaces and great temples (1 Kings 5:6; Psalms 7:2; Isaiah 2:13; Psalms 10:34). The divine voice that shatters what human greatness most prizes is the voice that Isaiah 40:6-8 describes as reducing all flesh to withering grass — "but the word of our God stands forever." Christ embodies and speaks this word: "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away" (Matthew 24:35). The voice that shatters cedar is also the voice that cannot be silenced even by death.
6: A theme: "He makes Lebanon skip like a calf, and Sirion like a young wild ox." The cosmic playfulness of the divine theophany — mountains "skipping" before the divine voice — reveals YHWH's sovereign relationship to the created order: even the most massive geological features are as lightweight before divine presence as calves before a human spectator. The NT's equivalent is Christ's sovereign authority over natural forces: calming the storm (Mark 4:39: the wind and sea obey his voice), walking on water (Mark 6:48-50), and the eschatological shaking of the heavens and earth at his return (Hebrews 12:26-27, citing Haggai 2:6). The creation that skips playfully before the divine voice in Ps 29 trembles before the returning Son of Man in the Gospels.
7: A theme: "The voice of the LORD strikes with flashes of lightning." Lightning as the weapon of divine speech and action — the divine voice made visible in the storm (Psalms 18:14; Psalms 97:4; Psalms 144:6). The book of Revelation presents the throne of God with "flashes of lightning, rumblings and peals of thunder" (Revelation 4:5), connecting the theophanic storm of Psalm 29 to the heavenly worship scene. The judgment-lightning associated with divine speech becomes, in the eschatological vision, the accompaniment of the Lamb who shares the divine throne. The voice that is lightning in Psalm 29 is the same voice that will judge the world at the last day (John 5:22, 27: "the Father has given all judgment to the Son").
8: A theme: "The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness; the LORD shakes the wilderness of Kadesh." The wilderness of Kadesh was the southern boundary of the promised land — where Israel wandered and where divine judgment fell (Num 20). The divine voice that "shakes the wilderness" (same verb as Hebrews 12:26's "I will shake the heavens and the earth once more") is the voice whose eschatological shaking Hebrews applies to the final judgment. Christ's voice is the agent of that final shaking: at his first coming he shook the wilderness of human sin by his presence; at his return he will shake all that can be shaken so that only the unshakeable kingdom remains (Hebrews 12:27-28).
9: A revelation of God: "The voice of the LORD causes the deer to writhe in labor and strips the forests bare; and in his temple all cry, 'Glory!'" The creation's involuntary response to the divine voice — the laboring deer, the stripped forests — gives way to the temple's unanimous cry of "glory" (kavod). In Revelation 5:12-13, the heavenly temple's doxology is directed to the Lamb: "Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and strength and honor and glory and praise!" The "glory" cry of the earthly temple in Psalms 29:9 reaches its eschatological form when the heavenly temple's entire population directs it to the crucified and risen Christ.
10: A theme: "The LORD sat enthroned over the Flood; the LORD sits enthroned as King forever." The divine throne above the flood-waters (Gen 6-8; Psalms 93:1-4) — YHWH's sovereignty over the chaos that threatened to undo creation — is exercised permanently ("King forever"). The NT applies this sovereign enthronement to Christ: the calming of the storm (Mark 4:39-41: "even the wind and the waves obey him!") is the disciples' first encounter with the one who sits enthroned above the waters; the resurrection and ascension are the demonstration that his kingship is permanent. Acts 2:33-36 announces the ascended Christ's enthronement at the right hand of the Father — the permanent Kingship of Psalms 29:10 given its final address.
11: A theme: "The LORD gives strength to his people; the LORD blesses his people with peace." The closing benediction of the theophanic psalm: the God whose voice terrifies the cosmos gives strength and peace to his covenant community. Christ embodies both gifts: in John 14:27, he gives his peace — "not as the world gives" — specifically to the disciples who are about to face the storm of his arrest and death. In Luke 24:49, he promises the "power (dynamis) from on high" — the strength the disciples will need after Pentecost. The terrifying divine voice of Psalm 29 is the same God who speaks peace and grants strength to those who are his — and the one through whom both blessings come is Christ.
